message: NSAIDs combined with anticoagulants significantly increase bleeding risk. NSAIDs inhibit platelet function and can cause gastrointestinal ulceration.
Concurrent NSAIDs and antiplatelet therapy markedly increases gastrointestinal bleeding risk.
Concurrent NSAIDs and steroids greatly increase the risk of peptic ulceration and gastrointestinal bleeding. Add gastric protection if co-prescribing.
SSRIs inhibit platelet serotonin uptake; combined with NSAIDs, gastrointestinal bleeding risk is significantly elevated.
NSAIDs can reduce the effectiveness of diuretics and worsen kidney function, especially in patients with compromised renal function.
NSAIDs can attenuate the antihypertensive effect of ACE inhibitors and increase nephrotoxicity risk.
FDA black box warning: concurrent opioids and benzodiazepines can cause profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death.
Concurrent opioids and sedatives potentiate CNS and respiratory depression. Combination should be avoided unless no alternatives exist.
Both agents cause CNS depression; concurrent use increases sedation and respiratory depression risk.
Tramadol combined with SSRIs can precipitate serotonin syndrome: agitation, confusion, tremor, tachycardia, hyperthermia.
Tramadol with antidepressants (especially serotonergic agents) increases risk of serotonin syndrome and seizures.
Combined use increases risk of respiratory depression and CNS depression.
Concurrent use of tramadol with other opioids dramatically increases respiratory depression and serotonin syndrome risk.
Dual anticoagulant and antiplatelet therapy substantially increases bleeding risk. Use only when clinical benefit clearly outweighs risk.
SSRIs can potentiate the anticoagulant effect of warfarin and increase bleeding risk.
Fluoroquinolones can enhance the anticoagulant effect of warfarin, increasing bleeding risk. Monitor INR closely.
Macrolide antibiotics (especially clarithromycin) inhibit CYP3A4 and can potentiate warfarin. Monitor INR.
Azole antifungals strongly inhibit CYP enzymes, substantially increasing warfarin levels and bleeding risk. Close INR monitoring required.
Concurrent benzodiazepines and sedatives cause additive CNS depression and respiratory depression.
Additive CNS depression; combination increases sedation, respiratory depression, and hypotension risk.
Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) combined with beta-blockers can cause severe bradycardia and heart block.
Beta-blockers mask the tachycardia warning signs of hypoglycemia, making it harder to detect low blood sugar in diabetic patients.
ACE inhibitors combined with potassium-sparing diuretics or potassium supplements can cause dangerous hyperkalaemia (high potassium).
ACE inhibitors with potassium supplements risk hyperkalaemia. Monitor potassium levels closely.
NSAIDs can reduce ACE inhibitor antihypertensive efficacy and increase nephrotoxicity risk (already listed from NSAID side).
Concurrent statin and fibrate therapy (especially gemfibrozil) significantly increases myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk.
Azole antifungals inhibit CYP3A4 and increase statin blood levels, raising the risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.
Macrolide antibiotics (especially clarithromycin) inhibit CYP3A4 and increase statin levels, raising myopathy risk.
Fluoroquinolones combined with NSAIDs may lower seizure threshold; the combination should be used with caution.
Both fluoroquinolones and antiarrhythmics can prolong the QT interval. Combination risks life-threatening torsades de pointes.
Both classes prolong QT interval; concurrent use significantly increases risk of torsades de pointes and cardiac arrest.
Macrolides can increase antipsychotic levels (CYP inhibition) and also prolong QT interval — additive QT prolongation risk.
Combining two serotonergic antidepressants risks serotonin syndrome. Avoid unless under specialist supervision.
Diuretics can alter serum electrolytes (sodium) which may affect seizure threshold and anticonvulsant effectiveness.
Loop diuretics can cause volume depletion and impair renal function, increasing the risk of metformin-associated lactic acidosis.
Azole antifungals markedly increase tacrolimus and cyclosporine levels via CYP inhibition — monitor drug levels and kidney function closely.
Enzyme-inducing anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin) reduce oral contraceptive efficacy, risking unintended pregnancy.
Enzyme-inducing anticonvulsants increase the metabolism of warfarin, reducing its effect. Monitor INR closely.
Some PPIs (especially omeprazole) may reduce the antiplatelet effect of clopidogrel via CYP2C19 inhibition. Consider pantoprazole as an alternative.
Concurrent aminoglycosides and loop diuretics greatly increase ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity risk.
NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow, increasing aminoglycoside accumulation and nephrotoxicity.
Already listed from anticoagulant side — azole antifungals strongly potentiate warfarin.
Already listed — non-dihydropyridine CCBs with beta-blockers risk bradycardia and heart block.
Already listed — additive respiratory and CNS depression.
Additive CNS depression with combined muscle relaxants and sedatives.
NSAIDs may enhance the hypoglycaemic effect of sulfonylureas by displacing them from protein binding or reducing renal excretion.
Fluoroquinolones can both increase and decrease blood glucose levels and may unpredictably alter sulfonylurea effect.
Already listed — loop diuretics with aminoglycosides increase ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.
First dose hypotension is possible when starting ACE inhibitors in patients already taking diuretics, especially if volume-depleted.

Patients with Penicillin/Amoxicillin allergy (Beta-lactam class) have a 1-10% cross-reactivity risk with Cephalosporins. Avoid unless benefits clearly outweigh risks and resuscitation facilities are available.

Cross-reactivity alert: if the patient has a documented allergy to any Beta-lactam antibiotic (Penicillin, Amoxicillin, Ampicillin), prescribing another Beta-lactam carries high anaphylaxis risk. Verify allergy history before prescribing.
Fluoroquinolones may enhance the anticoagulant effect of warfarin, increasing bleeding risk. Monitor INR closely if co-prescribing.
Clarithromycin and erythromycin inhibit CYP3A4, significantly raising statin plasma levels and myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk. Use azithromycin as a safer alternative.
Macrolides inhibit CYP3A4 and can increase warfarin effect. Monitor INR closely.
Broad-spectrum antibiotics disrupt gut flora, reducing vitamin K production and potentially enhancing warfarin anticoagulation. Monitor INR.
Absolute contraindication: Nitrates combined with PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) cause severe, potentially fatal hypotension. Do not co-prescribe.
Combining statins with fibrates (especially gemfibrozil) greatly increases myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk. Use with extreme caution and monitor CK levels.
Both ACE inhibitors and potassium-sparing diuretics raise serum potassium. Co-administration risks dangerous hyperkalaemia. Monitor potassium levels closely.
Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) combined with beta-blockers risk severe bradycardia, heart block, and hypotension.
Enzyme-inducing anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin) accelerate warfarin metabolism, reducing anticoagulant effect. INR may become erratic. Monitor closely.
